---
title: "Modelling TB Transmission Dynamics in Kenya (2000–2022)"
author: "Fiona Huini"
date: "2026-05-10"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    code-fold: true
    theme: cosmo
    self-contained: true

---

## Background


Tuberculosis (TB) remains a leading cause of infectious disease mortality globally, 
with Kenya classified among the 30 high TB burden countries by the World Health 
Organization (WHO). In 2022, Kenya reported an estimated incidence of 268 cases 
per 100,000 population, with treatment success rates of approximately 87% [@who2023].

A key feature of TB epidemiology is the long latent period between initial infection 
and progression to active disease. Only 5–10% of latently infected individuals 
develop active TB in their lifetime, with risk elevated by HIV co-infection, 
malnutrition, and immunosuppression [@pai2016]. This latent dynamic makes TB 
fundamentally different from directly transmitted diseases and requires explicit 
compartmental representation in transmission models.

Kenya's national TB program operates under the **End TB Strategy**, targeting a 
90% reduction in TB deaths and 80% reduction in incidence by 2030 relative to 
2015 baselines. Achieving these targets requires understanding the drivers of 
transmission and projecting the impact of intervention scale-up.

This analysis develops and calibrates a compartmental TB transmission model to 
Kenya's observed epidemic trajectory (2000–2022) and evaluates three intervention 
scenarios against End TB 2030 targets.
## Methods

###Model Structure

Here, we have developed a deterministic compartmental model with five states: 
Susceptible (**S**), Latent (**E**), Infectious (**I**), On Treatment (**T**), 
and Recovered (**R**). The model accounts for TB's characteristically long 
latent period and the possibility of relapse following recovery.

The system of ordinary differential equations (ODEs) governing transitions 
between compartments is implemented in `R/model.R` and solved numerically 
using the `deSolve` package.
## Results

### Baseline Simulation
```{r}
#| label: fig-baseline
#| fig-cap: "Baseline TB compartment dynamics in Kenya (2000–2022)"

source("R/model.R")
library(ggplot2)

# Parameters — literature values
params <- c(
  beta    = 0.15,   # NTLD Annual Report 2022
  k       = 0.10,   # NTLD Strategic Plan 2019-2023
  mu      = 0.0147, # KNBS Statistical Abstract 2023
  tau     = 0.68,   # NTLD Annual Report 2022
  delta   = 0.50,   # NTLD Annual Report 2021
  gamma_r = 0.75,   # NTLD Annual Report 2021
  rho     = 0.003   # NTLD Latent TB Policy 2020
)

# Compute and print R0 with literature parameters
R0_literature <- compute_R0(params)
cat("R0 with literature parameters:", round(R0_literature, 3), "\n")
N0 <- 31000000

initial_state <- c(
  S = N0 * 0.80,
  E = N0 * 0.15,
  I = N0 * 0.04,
  T = N0 * 0.005,
  R = N0 * 0.005
)

times <- seq(0, 22, by = 0.1)

result <- run_tb_model(params, initial_state, times)

library(tidyverse)

result |>
  pivot_longer(-time, names_to = "Compartment", values_to = "Count") |>
  ggplot(aes(x = time + 2000, y = Count / 1000, color = Compartment)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.9) +
  labs(
    x = "Year",
    y = "Population (thousands)",
    title = "TB Compartment Dynamics in Kenya 2000–2022",
    color = "Compartment"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```

## Discussion

## Limitations

## References
